title: What an American thinks about Serbia - bike touring may 2010

transcript.text: Hello. This video is what an American thinks about Bosnia. I was in Bosnia for maybe five days or so. I spent four days in Serbia. No, no, Serbia. What an American thinks about Serbia.
I was in Belgrade, Serbia, for four nights. I was there for four nights because I intended on riding my bicycle on the full moon for 20-30 hours straight.
I wasn't able to do that because I made myself very sick from eating too much McDonald's. Five Big Mark meals within two days.
And I also was drinking out of mouldy water bottles from the juice. I wasn't able to do that.
But my experiences of Bosnia. The Bosnians let me in. I think perhaps they asked me where I was going to go.
But they let me in. Right as I crossed the border, it started to rain.
My first experience of a Bosnia was of a little Roma child, about four years old, walked directly in front of me with his little doggy, his little puppy dog.
Literally right in front of me. Little cross-eyed boy.
Little cross-eyed boy. I literally had to stop almost all the way and go to the other side of the road or I would have killed a little boy.
And I went to Happy Hostel, which I was there for four nights.
And Belgrade is a very beautiful city.
It is right on the confluence of the Danube and the Rava rivers.
The Danube river was still quite large, even though it's much earlier in the Danube there than in Vienna.
No, no, no. Yeah, Vienna.
And so there's, you have the rivers and you have the lake right there, which was taken from the Rava, kind of a reservoir that is completely aligned with beaches.
And it's supposed to be the number one party city of all of Europe, according to Lonely Planet.
But Bosnians are very, very bad literatures.
There were lots of, as I was entering, not Bosnia, Serbia.
As I was entering Belgrade, I saw lots of fields of plastic bottles and trash and garbage, which I saw also along the banks of the river, the Rava river.
And also in the countryside.
Bosnians and Serbians, Serbians themselves seem to be very, of darker skin, darker complexion.
And I actually mentioned this to a boy on the street trying to ask me for money for the orphans.
And she said it was because of the, um, all of the Turks.
I said, are the Serbians related to the, uh, to the Roma?
And she said, no, no, no, no, it's because of the Turks from the last 400 years.
Um, so, um, I, I did the Wikipedia and it said that, uh, Siberia comes from the Serbians.
So perhaps the, the Serbians are perhaps nomadic, but, uh, Wikipedia said that they come originally from Iran and the, and the, the gypsies come from Iran.
So, um, perhaps they are, I would say they are related to the gypsies.
Um, Serbians, it seems to be more, less on the beaten path.
I was the only person, only of one of two people who I know of, or perhaps there were more.
But one other person in my, in my room the first night I was there, another man from Austin, Texas, which is where, where I am from.
Um, but then the last three nights I was there, I was the only person in the whole hostel.
So it's a little bit off the beaten track.
Uh, Serbia is quite cheap.
For two euros I bought a three-liter bottle of beer, um, which is about less than three dollars.
And, uh, but it was cheap beer.
I put it in my water bottle and it, uh, it immediately lined the water bottle with yeast hops.
It's a very dirty beer.
Um, Serbia, um, but the people seemed more excited to see me as a tourist.
They were honking their horns at me and, um, they're very friendly people.
Um, but I did notice a little bit of perhaps underhandedness, uh, dishonesty perhaps.
Uh, it is pretty much par for the course when you come to these poor countries.
That you're going to see behavior like this, where they are either stupid or they're dishonest.
It began in Croatia, where the man told me that breakfast was all day.
And then I, I go to breakfast and the man says, no breakfast has ended early.
So, that was the first example of the right hand not knowing what the left hand is doing.
And then another time I bought a, uh, mp3 player in Belgrade and the eight gigabyte memory card.
And when I got home, he had put the four gigabyte memory card in my bag.
So, I went back and I showed him the, the receipt, which said eight gigabytes and the four gigabyte that he gave me.
And he goes, oops, oh, sorry, so sorry, so sorry.
But he gave me a, uh, roll of tape.
So, perhaps it was just because he was stupid and not because he was dishonest.
There's really no way of telling.
But, um, the girls who worked at my hostel were quite pretty.
I think Bosnian women are supposed to be pretty because they have the dark skin, the good skin.
Um, and the girls who worked at my hostel were pretty.
One of them was very pretty.
Not particularly conversatory, though.
Uh, they watched a lot of TV and were on the computer a lot.
But their English was good.
The Bosnian English seems to be okay.
The standard Bosnian doesn't know English, but some of them do.
I would say it's about the same as, say, the Croatians and the, those other Eastern European countries, as terms of English.
One thing about the Europeans in general, though, all of the Europeans, is that they are chain smokers.
Americans don't smoke cigarettes anymore.
But the Europeans smoke lots and lots of the cigarettes.
Um, so, I enjoyed my time in Serbia.
I suppose that's all I have to say about Serbia.
There we go, I'll give you, uh, no way, in жизни.
Thanks for that, yeah.
I appreciate it.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
